topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elara stared at the mountain of papers spread across the kitchen table. Textbooks, crumpled worksheets, essays on the periodic table, even a forgotten doodle from third grade—all meticulously stacked. Her mother, usually a whirlwind of organized chaos, had apparently been hoarding academic documentation for decades. Elara tentatively picked up a heavily annotated history report. The ink was faded, the handwriting elegant and precise. A slow, disbelieving smile stretched across her face, quickly replaced by a deep furrow in her brow as she tried to process the sheer volume of preserved effort. "Mom?" she managed, the sound barely a whisper.

[story 2]

I found the box tucked behind the linen closet, and the contents made my stomach clench. It was a treasure trove: every graded paper, every homework assignment, every test booklet from my entire school career. I ran a hand over a stack of math proofs, the paper crisp and smelling faintly of old pencil shavings. My fingers traced the edges, a strange tension building in my chest. I looked toward the hallway, half-expecting my mother to materialize, looking flustered. Nothing. Just the silence of the house amplifying the weight of this unexpected archive of my past.

[story 3]

The sheer absurdity of it hit me like a physical blow. Hundreds of assignments, neatly filed by date, color-coded by subject. I sat back on my heels, my jaw slack. This wasn't just hoarding; this was an entire, tangible record of my formative years, curated by my mother. I picked up a science project from middle school, the glue still holding the cardboard structure together. My breath hitched. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small living room, my eyes darting from the papers to the empty doorway, a questioning tremor running through my hands. "What on earth is this?" I asked the empty air, my voice tight.
